oa7zxip.text: Totally fair concern. All the head tracking runs locally on the computer. No video is stored or sent anywhere. The webcam is only used to estimate the head position for the perspective effect.
oa7zxip.parent_text: This is badass. I however do not think many people want their video streaming into a random app unless it's open source / guaranteed to be secure.

oa7zq86.text: It tracks your head position with the webcam and adjusts the camera of a small 3D scene accordingly. So when you move left/right the perspective shifts, which creates the depth illusion. I explained it a bit more here:

oa7zsuh.text: Yes, the demo is a small 3D scene and the camera moves based on your head position.

oah7ai5.text: Thanks, I appreciate it! Performance on lower-end machines is something I’m actively testing and optimizing. Mobile/gyro support is also something I’d love to experiment with later. If you’re curious there’s a demo and some details here:
oah7ai5.parent_text: This is really impressive technical work! The head-tracking parallax effect is incredibly smooth. Using WebGL/ for this kind of head-pose estimation + perspective manipulation is the right call performance-wise. A few questions that might help as you develop HollowScope further: - Performance on lower-end devices? Head tracking + rendering effects can get heavy - Mobile support? Phone gyroscope for mobile devices could be next frontier - Battery drain on the device running this? The polish is genuinely impressive for a side project. The transitions are buttery and the effect is mesmerizing. Definitely has commercial potential if you lean into VR/AR experiences.

oah76z9.text: Right now it tracks a single face, so if two people move in opposite directions it will usually follow whichever face gets detected first. If you’re curious I put a demo and some details here:

oa7ux01.text: This is genuinely clever. The parallax-through-a-window effect is something I have seen done in mobile wallpapers but rarely pulled off well on desktop. Have you considered using facial landmark detection instead of just head position? It might give you smoother tracking since you would have more stable reference points to work with.

oa7wkk8.text: So cool! I immediately thought of Severance. Giving those vibes.

oa804kz.text: The illusion definitely works, we did something like this using wiimotes back in the day. At the time head tracking worked but it wasn't really fast enough for what we wanted. I thought about doing this a hundred times and then I came to the reality that I'm never on my desktop. But the illusion works. The software is really cool. Good work! Now instead of doing this on your desktop, maybe you should put this in a frame and hang it on your wall and make the picture change perspective as you walk by and nobody realizes it's a display. Maybe you can introduce a raspberry pi with an integrated web camera. Something really flat and simple.

oa80wq7.text: This is a really cool idea. That kind of interactive, 3D effect is always satisfying to see. Reminds me of those old-school holographic trading cards, but way more advanced. As someone building I'm always fascinated by how interfaces can change our perception and behavior. You're literally changing how someone sees their environment with a head movement. In trading, the problem is often that our perception of the chart gets distorted by our own psychology, and we need tools to help us see it clearly again. What was the biggest technical hurdle you ran into while getting the motion tracking to feel responsive and natural?

oaauqzu.text: Very cool! Good luck with the project ⭐️

A few questions that might help as you develop HollowScope further: - Performance on lower-end devices? Head tracking + rendering effects can get heavy - Mobile support? Phone gyroscope for mobile devices could be next frontier - Battery drain on the device running this? The polish is genuinely impressive for a side project. The transitions are buttery and the effect is mesmerizing. Definitely has commercial potential if you lean into VR/AR experiences.

oadq9tq.text: I love it and would immediately hate it. Great work ❤️

oaptbdh.text: I wanted to do exactly that 3 months ago but gave up when i realized that the 3d effect doesn't work with both eyes open so i gave up really quickly before having a finished product. Seeing this excited me again and I eagerly signed up for testing, very excited to test it.

oas50dx.text: Phase 2 Ideas - I know it will be difficult. But do you think you can extend it to multi monitor setups. That would be neat Another thing that can be done is if you can extend it as a framework so that anyone can convert maybe a 360 image or a 3d render into this kind of wallpaper.

oavcj2r.text: Awesome! im intrigued to know how it works, is it an actual illusion or does it use the laptops camera to track motion position?

obxhsyg.text: I've never understood how these work, on this or on iphone. Is it using the camera to see where I'm looking from?

obxo90p.text: Thanks! That's awesome, I love this.

odw3hfp.text: Damn MAN, it is so cool!

od08txc.text: You should be on Steam. Just like wallpaper engine this would destroy the market there

oe807c7.text: This is a very useful tool. I can see myself using this daily. Keep it up!

oes5xmk.text: This is genuinely one of the coolest side projects I've seen on here. The parallax effect using head tracking is such a clever use of the front camera. Would love to see how it handles low light conditions — that's usually where face tracking gets shaky. Excited for the beta!

oeynuun.text: S good reminder to move your neck while working

oeyqsj3.text: Awesome! How did you go about building it?

ofi2ru1.text: That's amazing! How did you do that?

ofqjvf0.text: Finally a feasible side project, thats awesome. I dont think this would be distracting rather would be a bit of an entertainment when feeling too monotonous while at work

oehegqq.text: Woahh!! Damn, that's a good work

ocri43y.text: I feel trapped, can I at least get a gun or a grenade or something?

odg48rd.text: Woah that looks insanely smooth... wish I could try it on Mac! I did a similar thing with an eye-tracking A/B testing app I just released for iPhone a few weeks ago () that leverages ARKit. What a world we're living in....

oewe9ed.text: Good execution on the webcam tracking. If you’re looking for real feedback: the tech is a 10, but the market fit is currently a question mark. You’ve got the 97% of people saying 'cool,' but have you found the 3% who would actually pay for an immersive desktop environment?

oa60gze.text: Buddy, are you planning on open sourcing the code??
